questions.q: ACS altitude tolerance for steep turns?
choices: plus or minus 50 ft
plus or minus 100 ft
plus or minus 200 ft
plus or minus 150 ft
questions.explanation: ACS Task VIII.B: Maintain entry altitude within plus or minus 100 ft throughout the steep turn maneuver.

questions.q: Bank angle required for steep turns on the Private Pilot ACS?
choices: 30 degrees
40 degrees
45 degrees
60 degrees
questions.explanation: ACS Task VIII.B requires steep turns at exactly 45 degrees of bank. Load factor at 45 degrees is 1.41g.

questions.q: Vx is used for what purpose?
choices: Maximum range speed
Best angle of climb — maximum altitude gained per unit of horizontal distance
Best rate of climb — maximum altitude gained per unit of time
Turbulence penetration speed
questions.explanation: Vx (best angle of climb) provides maximum altitude gain per unit of horizontal distance. Used for obstacle clearance after takeoff. Transition to Vy once clear.

questions.q: Vy is used for what purpose?
choices: Best angle of climb for obstacle clearance
Best rate of climb — maximum altitude gain per unit of time
Normal approach speed
Maneuvering speed for turbulence
questions.explanation: Vy (best rate of climb) provides maximum altitude gain per unit of time. Used for en-route climb after obstacle clearance to reach cruise altitude efficiently.

questions.q: First action in any stall recovery?
choices: Apply full power immediately
Retract flaps immediately
Reduce angle of attack by pitching forward to break the stall
Apply right rudder to stop roll
questions.explanation: First priority in stall recovery is to reduce the angle of attack — pitch the nose forward to break the stall. Apply full power after the stall breaks and retract flaps incrementally.

questions.q: Normal landing ACS touchdown tolerance?
choices: plus or minus 100 ft
plus or minus 200 ft
plus or minus 400 ft
plus or minus 600 ft
questions.explanation: ACS Task X.A: Normal landing requires touchdown within plus or minus 400 ft of the aiming point, on centerline, with no drift, at or below Vref.

questions.q: Power-off stall recovery — when should the pilot initiate recovery?
choices: After aircraft loses 100 ft of altitude
At the first indication of stall — buffet, stall horn, or loss of control effectiveness
Only after a full stall break occurs
At a specific airspeed listed in the POH
questions.explanation: ACS requires recovery at the first indication of stall: stall warning horn activation, buffet, or loss of normal control effectiveness. Do not wait for a full stall.

questions.q: In ground reference maneuvers, when should bank angle be steepest?
choices: When heading directly into the wind
When heading directly crosswind
When heading downwind with highest groundspeed
Bank angle is always constant throughout
questions.explanation: To maintain constant radius ground track: downwind = highest groundspeed = steepest bank needed. Upwind = lowest groundspeed = shallowest bank.

questions.q: Entry position for turns around a point?
choices: Directly upwind of the point
45 degrees to the downwind leg abeam the point
On a downwind heading directly abeam the point at desired radius
Directly crosswind of the point
questions.explanation: Standard entry: on a downwind heading directly abeam the point at the desired radius distance. This ensures the steepest bank is needed at entry when groundspeed is highest.

questions.q: Soft field takeoff elevator control input during the takeoff roll?
choices: Neutral elevator
Full forward elevator to reduce nosewheel drag
Full aft (back) pressure on elevator to transfer weight to wings and off the nosewheel
Differential braking only with neutral elevator
questions.explanation: Full aft elevator pressure reduces weight on the nosegear (which could dig into soft surface) and transfers weight to the wings as quickly as possible for early liftoff.

questions.q: Short field landing ACS touchdown tolerance?
choices: plus or minus 100 ft
At or within 200 ft beyond the aiming point
plus or minus 400 ft
At or before the aiming point
questions.explanation: ACS Task X.F: Short field landing requires touchdown at or within 200 ft beyond the specified touchdown point. More demanding than normal landing (400 ft).

questions.q: Correct go-around sequence after a full-flaps approach?
choices: Apply full flaps then power then pitch up
Full power then carb heat off then retract all flaps at once then Vy
Pitch for climb attitude plus full power then carb heat off then flaps incrementally then Vy climb
Retract all flaps first then apply power
questions.explanation: Go-around: simultaneously pitch for climb attitude and apply full power, turn off carb heat, retract flaps incrementally per POH (not all at once), then establish Vy climb speed.

questions.q: During S-turns across a road when should the wings be level?
choices: Only at the initial entry point
Each time the aircraft crosses the reference line (the road)
Only when heading directly upwind
Only at the midpoint of each semi-circle
questions.explanation: Wings must be level each time the aircraft crosses the reference road. Immediately after crossing the bank increases to its steepest (downwind side) then progressively decreases as the aircraft turns upwind.

questions.q: Load factor at 45 degrees bank and effect on stall speed?
choices: 1.0g — no effect on stall speed
1.41g — stall speed increases by approximately 19 percent
2.0g — stall speed doubles
1.41g — stall speed increases by approximately 41 percent
questions.explanation: Load factor at 45 degrees = 1 divided by cosine of 45 = 1.41g. Stall speed increases by the square root of 1.41 which is approximately 1.19 — about a 19 percent increase. At 60 degrees: LF = 2.0g, stall speed +41%.

questions.q: Why does slow flight require more right rudder than cruise flight?
choices: The rudder is physically less effective at slow airspeeds
P-factor, torque, and spiraling slipstream all increase at high angle of attack and low airspeed causing a left-turning tendency that must be countered
The aileron takes over rudder function at slow speed
More rudder is simply needed for coordination at any slow speed
questions.explanation: The four left-turning tendencies (P-factor, torque, spiraling slipstream, gyroscopic precession) are most pronounced at high angle of attack and low airspeed — exactly slow flight conditions. Right rudder counters these forces.

questions.q: First action after an engine failure in flight?
choices: Declare an emergency on 121.5 MHz
Attempt engine restart immediately using the emergency checklist
Establish best glide speed (Vg or Vbg) immediately to maximize gliding range
Identify and turn toward the nearest airport
questions.explanation: First priority: establish best glide speed immediately to maximize gliding range and time available. Then: choose a landing site, run the restart checklist, communicate (squawk 7700, 121.5 MHz), and prepare for off-airport landing.

questions.q: What is ground effect and how does it benefit soft field takeoff?
choices: Friction of the wheels on the runway surface
Reduction of induced drag when flying within approximately one wingspan height AGL — allows acceleration to Vy in the reduced-drag environment
Effect of crosswind components near the surface
Turbulence created by heated ground on warm days
questions.explanation: Ground effect within approximately one wingspan height AGL reduces induced drag up to 50 percent. In a soft field takeoff, the aircraft stays in ground effect to accelerate to Vy before climbing away.

questions.q: Forward slip is used for what purpose?
choices: Increasing airspeed on final approach
Rapidly losing altitude without increasing airspeed — useful when overshooting the glidepath or landing without flaps
Correcting for crosswind during landing — same as a sideslip
Increasing lift during short field operations
questions.explanation: A forward slip creates significant drag through cross-controlling (aileron deflection and opposite rudder), which bleeds altitude without increasing airspeed. Useful when overshooting or when flaps are unavailable.

questions.q: Crosswind landing using the wing-low (sideslip) technique — correct control inputs?
choices: Into-wind aileron plus opposite downwind rudder to align the nose with the runway
Opposite aileron (upwind) plus opposite rudder — both away from wind
Both aileron and rudder input into the wind simultaneously
Crab into the wind throughout and remove the crab just before touchdown
questions.explanation: Wing-low (sideslip): apply into-wind aileron (upwind wing drops slightly) plus downwind rudder (to align nose with runway centerline). The upwind main gear touches down first.

questions.q: The aircraft ahead executes a go-around. Their vortices drift across your flight path at your altitude on final. Best action?
choices: Continue the approach — wake turbulence only affects aircraft directly below
Stay on centerline — vortices dissipate within 30 seconds
Execute a go-around or fly on the upwind side of the runway centerline to avoid the descending and drifting vortices
Increase airspeed to fly through the wake turbulence zone quickly
questions.explanation: Wake vortices sink at 400 to 500 fpm and drift downwind. If a preceding aircraft goes around ahead of you, their vortices are at approximately your altitude and drifting. Go-around or fly the upwind side of their track.

questions.q: What is the base-to-final stall and what causes it?
choices: A stall caused by excessive speed on final approach
An accelerated stall caused by excessive bank, back pressure, and skidding turn when overshooting the centerline at low altitude
A stall caused by flap deployment on final
A stall caused by flying too slow on the base leg
questions.explanation: The base-to-final stall occurs when overshooting the centerline causes the pilot to add back pressure and skid the turn with outside rudder rather than increasing bank. This creates an accelerated stall at very low altitude.

questions.q: What is a secondary stall?
choices: A stall that occurs on the second attempt at landing
A stall during recovery from the initial stall when the pilot pulls back too aggressively before sufficient airspeed is regained
A stall in the traffic pattern below 600 ft AGL
A stall caused by ice accumulation on the wings
questions.explanation: A secondary stall occurs during recovery from an initial stall when back pressure is applied too aggressively before adequate airspeed returns, again exceeding the critical angle of attack.

questions.q: What is wake turbulence and what generates it?
choices: Engine exhaust turbulence trailing behind the aircraft
Wingtip vortices generated whenever a wing produces lift — counter-rotating vortices trailing behind the aircraft
Propwash from large propeller-driven aircraft
Jet blast from jet engines at low power settings
questions.explanation: Wake turbulence is generated by the production of lift. High pressure air beneath the wing rolls around the wingtip to low pressure above, creating counter-rotating vortices. Heaviest aircraft at slowest speed produces the worst wake.

questions.q: ACS heading tolerance during straight-and-level flight?
choices: plus or minus 5 degrees
plus or minus 10 degrees
plus or minus 15 degrees
plus or minus 20 degrees
questions.explanation: ACS Task V.A: Straight-and-level flight tolerance is heading plus or minus 10 degrees, altitude plus or minus 100 ft, and airspeed plus or minus 10 kts.

questions.q: During an emergency landing off-airport what radio and transponder actions should be taken?
choices: No radio action needed — focus on flying only
Squawk 7700 and attempt to contact ATC on 121.5 MHz while continuing to fly the aircraft
Only contact tower if one is nearby
File a flight plan on 122.8 MHz
questions.explanation: When declaring an emergency: squawk 7700 on the transponder and attempt to contact ATC on 121.5 MHz. Aviate first — navigate and communicate are secondary priorities.

questions.q: Short field takeoff — what speed is used for initial climb to clear an obstacle?
choices: Vy — best rate of climb
Va — maneuvering speed
Vx — best angle of climb until obstacle is cleared then transition to Vy
Normal rotation speed plus 10 knots
questions.explanation: Short field takeoff: rotate at the POH-specified speed, climb at Vx (best angle) to clear the obstacle, then transition to Vy for the en-route climb. Always use the POH for specific speeds.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the rectangular course ground reference maneuver?
choices: To practice steep turns at altitude
To simulate flying the airport traffic pattern and practice wind drift correction on straight legs and turns
To practice navigation using ground references
To develop proficiency in short field operations
questions.explanation: The rectangular course simulates the traffic pattern and develops the pilot's ability to make proper wind drift corrections on straight legs and coordinate turns of varying bank to maintain a rectangular ground track.

questions.q: How high AGL should ground reference maneuvers generally be performed?
choices: 300 to 500 ft AGL
600 to 1000 ft AGL
1500 to 2000 ft AGL
2500 to 3000 ft AGL
questions.explanation: ACS and AFH specify ground reference maneuvers should be performed at 600 to 1000 ft AGL. This provides adequate safety margin while keeping the aircraft close enough to observe ground references clearly.

questions.q: What speed should be established immediately after an engine failure?
choices: Vx — best angle of climb to reach the nearest airport
Best glide speed (Vg or Vbg) to maximize gliding range and time available
Vy — best rate of climb speed
Va — maneuvering speed to protect the airframe
questions.explanation: Immediately establish best glide speed. In most small aircraft this is listed in the POH. It provides the maximum gliding distance for the altitude available.

questions.q: During a power-on departure stall why is right rudder critical?
choices: Right rudder is not needed during departure stalls
At high power and high angle of attack, left-turning tendencies (P-factor, torque, slipstream) are at maximum and must be countered aggressively to prevent a snap roll or spin entry
Right rudder prevents the flaps from extending asymmetrically
Right rudder helps the aircraft climb at Vx during recovery
questions.explanation: During power-on stalls at high angle of attack and full power, all four left-turning tendencies are at maximum strength. Without aggressive right rudder, the aircraft may snap left and enter an incipient spin — extremely dangerous at low altitude.

questions.q: What is Va (maneuvering speed) and why does it change with aircraft weight?
choices: Va is the maximum structural cruising speed — fixed by design and does not change with weight because the aircraft's certified limit load factor is constant regardless of fuel state, passenger loading, or cargo configuration.

Va is the maximum speed at which full or abrupt application of any single flight control will not cause structural damage. Va decreases as weight decreases because a lighter aircraft develops less lift per unit deflection and stalls sooner, protecting the structure.

Va is the turbulence-penetration speed recommended for flight in rough air — it increases as weight decreases because a lighter airframe requires more airspeed to generate the same structural margin against gust loads at altitude.

Va is the maximum flap extension speed; it decreases as aircraft weight increases because a heavier aircraft generates more lift from flap deflection and risks exceeding the wing's limit-load factor at lower indicated airspeeds.
questions.explanation: Va (maneuvering speed) is the maximum speed at which full deflection of a single flight control will not overstress the aircraft (the aircraft will stall before exceeding limit load). Va decreases as weight decreases — a lighter aircraft stalls more easily providing structural protection. Always use the POH Va for current aircraft weight.

questions.q: During steep turns, why does the nose tend to drop and what is the corrective action?
choices: The nose drops because carburetor ice forms during prolonged turns, leaning the mixture and reducing engine power output. Applying carb heat restores power and allows the pilot to maintain altitude and bank angle through the steep turn.

The nose drops because the increased load factor (1.41g at 45 degrees) requires increased lift, which means increased back pressure on the elevator is needed throughout the turn. As bank increases, more back pressure is required.

The nose drops because induced drag increases significantly at the 45-degree bank angle, reducing airspeed and angle of attack. The pilot must add power simultaneously with back pressure to maintain both altitude and bank angle.

The nose drops because adverse yaw at 45 degrees deflects the nose below the turn horizon, requiring rudder correction opposite the bank direction. As bank increases, more opposite rudder is needed to prevent altitude loss.
questions.explanation: In a steep turn, the increased load factor (1.41g at 45 degrees) means the aircraft requires more lift than in level flight. This increased lift requirement must be provided by increasing angle of attack through back pressure on the elevator. If back pressure is not maintained, the nose will drop and altitude will be lost.

questions.q: What is the purpose of performing clearing turns before beginning a maneuver?
choices: Clearing turns warm up the engine before high-power maneuvers
Clearing turns are 90-degree or 180-degree turns performed before practicing maneuvers to clear the area of other aircraft — standard practice before any slow flight, stall, or steep turn
Clearing turns reset the heading indicator
Clearing turns are required by the ACS in all maneuvers
questions.explanation: Clearing turns (typically two 90-degree turns or one 180-degree turn) are performed before any maneuver that diverts attention from outside (steep turns, slow flight, stalls). They allow the pilot to visually clear the practice area of other traffic. This is standard safe practice and expected during the checkride.

questions.q: What is an accelerated stall and how is it different from a power-off stall?
choices: An accelerated stall occurs only at very high airspeeds, where excessive dynamic pressure overloads the elevator and forces an abrupt pitch-up, driving the wing past the critical angle of attack before the pilot can react

An accelerated stall occurs when the critical angle of attack is exceeded while the aircraft is in a banked or maneuvering condition — the stall can occur at speeds significantly above the normal stall speed due to the increased load factor from the maneuver

An accelerated stall is triggered by rapid throttle application during climb, which increases propeller torque and p-factor, rotating the nose up past the critical angle of attack regardless of airspeed or bank angle

An accelerated stall results from exceeding the aircraft's design load factor during level flight cruise, causing airflow separation at the wing root before the pilot senses any buffet or stick-force increase
questions.explanation: An accelerated stall occurs when the critical angle of attack is reached while the aircraft is under increased load factor (in a turn, pull-up, etc.). Because load factor increases the stall speed (Vs x sqrt(LF)), an accelerated stall can occur at airspeeds well above the normal Vs. The base-to-final stall is a classic example.

questions.q: What is the proper technique for establishing and maintaining slow flight?
choices: Immediately reduce power to idle and hold altitude with back pressure; deploy full flaps simultaneously to increase lift and then trim to the published slow-flight speed while maintaining wings level with rudder inputs.

Gradually reduce power while maintaining altitude with increasing back pressure, extend flaps as needed, add right rudder to counteract left-turning tendencies. Establish at a speed 5-10 kts above stall. Maintain altitude with back pressure and power adjustments.

Reduce power to the approach setting, lower the nose to maintain airspeed, then extend flaps incrementally until reaching the stall speed; hold that speed by adding left rudder to counter propeller torque at high angle of attack.

Apply carburetor heat first, then reduce power smoothly while adding forward pressure to maintain altitude; once flaps are fully extended, trim nose-down and use aileron inputs to hold the target airspeed above Va.
questions.explanation: Slow flight setup: gradually reduce power while maintaining altitude with back pressure, add flaps incrementally, add right rudder to compensate for increased left-turning tendencies at high AoA. Establish and maintain flight at 5-10 kts above stall speed. The aircraft should be responsive to all controls (not in a mushing condition).

questions.q: During a power-on stall (departure stall), what control input is most critical to prevent an incipient spin?
choices: Elevator — maintain back pressure throughout
Aileron — use aileron to keep wings level
Rudder — aggressive right rudder to counteract left-turning tendencies at full power and high AoA. A wing drop during the stall must be corrected with rudder, not aileron.
Throttle — reduce power at first stall indication
questions.explanation: During power-on stalls, all four left-turning tendencies are at maximum intensity. Aggressive right rudder is critical to keep the ball centered and prevent a wing from dropping. If a wing drops during the stall, use opposite RUDDER (not aileron) to raise it — aileron input during a stall can aggravate a spin.

questions.q: What is the standard traffic pattern altitude for propeller-driven aircraft at an airport without a published pattern altitude?
choices: 500 ft AGL
800 ft AGL
1000 ft AGL
1500 ft AGL
questions.explanation: The standard traffic pattern altitude for propeller-driven aircraft is 1000 ft AGL unless otherwise specified in the Chart Supplement or by NOTAMs. Turbine-powered aircraft typically fly 1500 ft AGL. Pattern altitudes may vary and should always be checked in the Chart Supplement.

questions.q: What is the proper scanning technique to detect other aircraft in VFR conditions?
choices: Continuous left-to-right sweeping from wingtip to wingtip at a brisk, steady pace — the eye tracks moving objects best during a smooth sweep across the entire visual field without pausing on any single sector.

Systematic sectional scanning — divide the field of view into segments of 10-15 degrees and pause briefly on each sector. The eye detects movement and change best when not sweeping continuously. Also check different vertical levels.

Focus scanning on the 12 o'clock position and traffic pattern area; the greatest collision risk is directly ahead, so spending most of your visual attention forward reduces exposure during cruise and approach phases.

Use TCAS and ADS-B traffic displays as the primary collision-avoidance tool; visual scanning is unreliable beyond 3 miles and should supplement electronic traffic alerting rather than replace it during VFR cruise.
questions.explanation: The most effective collision avoidance technique is systematic sectional scanning. Divide the visual field into 10-15 degree segments and pause momentarily on each. The eye detects movement/contrast changes most effectively when paused. Continuous sweeping reduces detection ability. Also look high and low, not just level.

questions.q: What is the effect of carburetor ice on engine operation and how is it recognized?
choices: Carb ice increases engine roughness and power but does not affect RPM

Carb ice causes a gradual unexplained decrease in RPM (fixed-pitch prop) or manifold pressure (constant-speed prop), often accompanied by engine roughness. In severe cases, the engine may quit entirely.
Carb ice causes the fuel gauge to drop rapidly
Carb ice only affects magneto-driven engines
questions.explanation: Carburetor ice restricts airflow through the carburetor, leaning the mixture and reducing engine power. Symptoms: gradual decrease in RPM (fixed-pitch) or manifold pressure (constant-speed), possible roughness. Apply full carb heat — expect further RPM drop as ice melts and enrichens mixture, then smooth RPM increase as ice clears.

questions.q: During a normal approach and landing, what is the purpose of the final flap extension?
choices: Final flap extension increases airspeed for safety
Final flap extension increases drag and lift, allowing a steeper approach path at a slower airspeed — reduces the landing roll and allows precise glidepath control
Final flap extension is only for short field landings
Full flaps are never used in normal landings
questions.explanation: Extending flaps (particularly the final increment) on final approach increases both lift and drag. This allows the aircraft to maintain a steeper glidepath at a slower approach speed, reducing float over the runway and landing roll. The specific flap setting and approach speed are determined by the POH.

questions.q: What is Va (maneuvering speed) and how should it be used in turbulence?
choices: Maximum flap extension speed
Design maneuvering speed — at or below Va, full deflection of a single control will not overstress the airframe; slow to Va or below in turbulence
Minimum control airspeed
Best glide speed for emergencies
questions.explanation: Va is the design maneuvering speed. At or below Va, a full deflection of one control surface will not overstress the airframe — the aircraft stalls before structural damage occurs. In turbulence, reduce to Va. Note: Va decreases as weight decreases.

questions.q: What is Vfe (flap extension speed) and why is it important?
choices: The speed at which flaps must be extended on all approaches
Maximum speed at which flaps may be extended — exceeding Vfe can damage the flap mechanism; indicated by top of white arc on ASI
Minimum speed for flap extension
Optimal speed for short field landings
questions.explanation: Vfe is the maximum flap extended speed — the top of the white arc on the airspeed indicator. Extending flaps or flying with flaps extended above Vfe can cause structural damage to the flap system. Always verify airspeed before extending flaps.

questions.q: What is the normal traffic pattern sequence at a non-towered airport?
choices: Take off, fly direct to final, land
Upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final — left pattern unless right traffic is specified
Downwind, crosswind, upwind, base, final
Direct entry to any leg is preferred
questions.explanation: Standard traffic pattern: Upwind (parallel to runway in takeoff direction), Crosswind (90-degree turn), Downwind (parallel, opposite landing direction), Base (90-degree turn), Final (aligned with runway). Standard pattern is left-turning unless right traffic is specified.

questions.q: What is traffic pattern altitude (TPA) at most airports?
choices: Always 1000 ft AGL
500 ft AGL
Typically 1000 ft AGL for piston aircraft; 1500 ft AGL for turbine aircraft; verify in Chart Supplement for specific airport
2000 ft AGL standard
questions.explanation: Standard TPA is 1000 ft AGL for piston aircraft and 1500 ft AGL for turbine aircraft. Some airports have non-standard TPAs due to obstacles, noise abatement, or other factors. Always check the Chart Supplement for each airport's published TPA.

questions.q: What is a stabilized approach and why is it critical?
choices: An approach where the autopilot maintains glidepath and airspeed from the final approach fix to the threshold — manually flown approaches are by definition unstabilized and require a go-around if deviations exceed one dot.

An approach where the aircraft is on the correct flight path at target airspeed in the correct configuration with stable power by 500 ft AGL — unstabilized approaches are a leading cause of landing accidents; go-around if not stabilized

An approach where the aircraft crosses the outer marker on glidepath; minor speed or configuration deviations below 1000 ft AGL are acceptable as long as the runway environment is in sight and a normal landing is assured.

An approach flown in VMC where the pilot maintains a constant visual glidepath angle of 3 degrees without reference to instruments — acceptable deviations are plus or minus 10 knots and one full dot of glidepath deflection.
questions.explanation: A stabilized approach requires by 500 ft AGL (VFR): correct glidepath, target airspeed +/-5 kts, landing configuration, stable power. Unstabilized approaches are a leading cause of approach and landing accidents. If not stabilized by the gate, execute a go-around.

questions.q: What two conditions must be present simultaneously for a spin to develop?
choices: High power and low airspeed
A stalled condition AND a yawing motion (usually from uncoordinated rudder)
A bank angle exceeding 45 degrees with back pressure
High altitude and steep descent angle
questions.explanation: A spin requires: (1) a stalled wing — the aircraft must be at or beyond the critical angle of attack, AND (2) a yawing motion — typically from uncoordinated rudder (skid). The yaw causes one wing to stall more deeply than the other, creating the differential lift that initiates autorotation.

questions.q: What does PARE stand for in spin recovery?
choices: Pull-Altitude-Roll-Exit
Power-Ailerons-Rudder-Elevator
Power idle, Ailerons neutral, Rudder full opposite to spin direction, Elevator forward to break the stall
Pitch-Airspeed-Recovery-Exit
questions.explanation: PARE spin recovery: Power to idle (prevents worsening spin), Ailerons neutralized (ailerons can worsen spin), Rudder full opposite to rotation direction (stops autorotation), Elevator briskly forward (breaks the stall). Once rotation stops: neutralize rudder, recover from dive smoothly.

questions.q: Why should ailerons be neutralized rather than applied into the spin during spin recovery?
choices: Aileron application is not possible during a spin due to airflow
In some aircraft configurations, applying aileron into or away from the spin can increase the rate of rotation or aggravate the spin — PARE requires neutral ailerons to avoid this risk
Ailerons have no effect during a spin in any phase
Ailerons must be applied with the spin to maintain coordination
questions.explanation: During a spin, the aerodynamic effects of aileron deflection are different from normal flight. In some aircraft, applying aileron can increase the rate of rotation (pro-spin). PARE specifies neutral ailerons to eliminate this variable. Rudder is the primary control for stopping autorotation; elevator breaks the stall.

questions.q: What is the correct sequence for recovering from a nose-low unusual attitude?
choices: Pitch up immediately, then add power, then roll wings level
Power to idle, roll wings level, then smoothly raise the nose to level flight attitude
Add full power, apply back pressure, then roll wings level
Roll into a steep bank to slow the nose-low descent then recover
questions.explanation: Nose-low unusual attitude recovery: (1) Power to idle to prevent overspeed, (2) Level the wings (this is critical — pulling while banked loads the aircraft heavily), (3) Smoothly raise the nose to level. Do NOT pull back before leveling wings or you risk an accelerated stall or Vne exceedance.

questions.q: What instruments remain functional when the vacuum system fails in flight?
choices: All six primary flight instruments remain functional
Only the altimeter remains functional
Turn coordinator (electric gyro), airspeed indicator, altimeter, and VSI remain functional — attitude indicator and heading indicator become unreliable
Only the magnetic compass and altimeter are reliable
questions.explanation: Vacuum failure makes the attitude indicator and heading indicator unreliable within minutes. Instruments that still work: turn coordinator (electric gyro), airspeed indicator, altimeter, VSI (all pitot-static or electric), and magnetic compass. Transition to partial panel techniques using these instruments.

questions.q: What is the correct recovery procedure for a nose-high unusual attitude?
choices: Power to idle, then lower the nose, then roll wings level
Add full power, roll wings level, then lower the nose to level flight attitude
Simultaneously add power and push the nose down
Apply back pressure to prevent further pitch increase, then roll wings level
questions.explanation: Nose-high unusual attitude: (1) Add full power (aircraft is decelerating and may be near stall), (2) Level the wings, (3) Lower the nose to level flight attitude. Memory: nose-HIGH = power HIGH. The aircraft needs energy to avoid a stall — power first, then level, then pitch.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a forward slip to landing?
choices: To increase airspeed on final approach to prevent stall
To rapidly lose altitude without increasing airspeed — useful when high on approach with insufficient runway remaining or when flaps are unavailable
To correct for a crosswind during the final approach — same as a sideslip
To increase engine cooling during descent
questions.explanation: A forward slip creates significant drag by cross-controlling (opposite aileron and rudder), which bleeds altitude quickly without increasing airspeed. It is useful when overshooting the glidepath or when flaps cannot be used. The nose is pointed to the side while the flight path continues toward the runway.

questions.q: What POH limitation must be checked before performing a forward slip with full flaps?
choices: No limitation exists — forward slips are always permitted with any flap setting

Check the POH — some aircraft prohibit slips with certain flap configurations. The Cessna 172 POH notes that forward slips with full flaps may result in a temporary reduction in elevator effectiveness.
Forward slips are only prohibited without flaps
The limitation only applies to slips exceeding 30 seconds
questions.explanation: POH must always be checked. The Cessna 172 POH notes: 'Forward slips with full flaps and high power settings may result in a tendency for the aircraft to pitch up.' Some aircraft prohibit slips with full flaps. Airspeed indication may also be unreliable during a slip due to pitot tube positioning.

questions.q: In the wing-low (sideslip) crosswind landing technique, what is the correct control input?
choices: Aileron into the wind and same-direction rudder to align with the runway
Aileron into the wind (upwind wing drops) and opposite (downwind) rudder to align the nose with the runway centerline
Both aileron and rudder away from the wind direction
Crab into the wind throughout and remove at the last moment
questions.explanation: Wing-low (sideslip): apply upwind aileron (lowers the upwind wing to prevent drift) and opposite downwind rudder (aligns the nose with the runway). The aircraft touches down on the upwind main gear first with no side load. The ball will be off-center during this maneuver — this is intentional and correct.
